beats.text: An autoencoder is trained to copy its input to its output, a task that only becomes useful once you choke the path between them.
An encoder maps the input down to a narrow code, and a decoder maps that code back up to a reconstruction trained to match the input.
The bottleneck makes a perfect copy impossible, so the network must spend its few code dimensions on whatever explains the most variance.
A 28 by 28 image has 784 pixels, but handwritten digits occupy a far thinner sheet inside that 784-dimensional cube.
With a linear encoder and squared-error loss, the optimum spans exactly the top-k principal components, so a linear autoencoder rediscovers PCA.
A bottleneck is one way to stop an autoencoder from learning the identity map, but it is not the only way and not always the best.
If the code is as wide as the input, a vanilla autoencoder can cheat by copying the input straight through and learning nothing.
A denoising autoencoder corrupts the input with noise and then demands a clean reconstruction, so copying is no longer possible.
To undo corruption the denoiser learns the score, the gradient of the log-density pointing back toward where real data lives.

visual.note: the learned score, the seed of diffusion

beats.text: Sparse autoencoders penalize how many units fire at once, and contractive autoencoders penalize the encoder Jacobian to stay locally invariant.
All four variants share one moral: an autoencoder is only as good as the pressure you put on its code.
The code is not just a compressed file, it is a place: the set of all codes the encoder can produce is the latent space.
Distances in latent space track perceptual and semantic distance far better than raw pixel distance does.
You can interpolate between two codes and decode the path to morph smoothly from one example to another.
A point the autoencoder reconstructs poorly is off the learned manifold, so reconstruction error is an unsupervised novelty score.
But a plain autoencoder's latent has holes: decode a random midpoint and you often get garbage, which is the entire motivation for the VAE.
The variational autoencoder closes the holes by making the encoder output a distribution instead of a single point.
The encoder emits a mean and a variance defining a Gaussian, and the loss regularizes that toward a standard normal prior.
The objective is the evidence lower bound: a reconstruction term in tension with a KL divergence pulling the code toward the prior.

visual.tex: \log p_\theta(x) \ge \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z\mid x)}\!\big[\log p_\theta(x\mid z)\big] - D_{\mathrm{KL}}\!\big(q_\phi(z\mid x)\,\Vert\,p(z)\big)

beats.text: For diagonal Gaussians the KL term has a clean closed form that is zero exactly when each dimension matches the prior.

visual.note: KL of diagonal Gaussian to N(0,I)

beats.text: A unit dimension with mean one and variance one costs exactly half a nat, since the variance matches and the mean is one deviation off.
The reparameterization trick moves randomness outside the network so gradients flow through the mean and variance cleanly.
Autoencoders matter today less as standalone generators and more as representation learners that feed everything downstream.
Train an encoder unsupervised on unlabelled data, then attach a small head and fine-tune; masked autoencoding made this the dominant vision recipe.
Train on normal data only and flag inputs the model reconstructs poorly, catching fraud, defects, intrusions, and equipment faults.
Stable Diffusion runs its expensive denoising inside a VAE-compressed latent grid, which is why a consumer GPU can generate megapixel images.
VAE samples are notoriously blurry, and posterior collapse can drive the latent to carry no information at all.
The VAE's enduring win is not photorealism but a well-organized, sampleable latent space, exactly what the generative stack needed.
